title: Dog Walking Unsteadily or Falling Over: Triage & Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog wobbly or falling? Learn why unsteady walking is a high-urgency veterinary emergency and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden unsteadiness or "drunken" walking in dogs is a high-urgency emergency that requires immediate veterinary evaluation to diagnose potentially life-threatening underlying conditions.

What is ataxia and how does it affect my dog's movement?

When a dog walks unsteadily, stumbles, or falls over, it is medically referred to as ataxia. This condition is not a disease itself but a symptom of an underlying issue affecting the nervous system, inner ear, or musculoskeletal system. It often appears as 'drunken' walking, swaying, or a complete loss of balance.

Is my dog's sudden unsteadiness a medical emergency?

Yes. Sudden unsteadiness or the inability to stand is a High-Urgency emergency. These symptoms can be caused by various serious conditions, including:

  • Stroke
  • Spinal cord injury
  • Toxin ingestion
  • Vestibular disease

Because some causes can be life-threatening or lead to permanent paralysis, immediate veterinary evaluation is necessary.

Why should I take a photo or video of my dog to help with triage?

Capturing a short video of your dog's gait and eye movements is incredibly helpful for the triage process. It helps the veterinary team by:

  • Providing clear evidence of the severity and specific type of incoordination
  • Identifying specific signs like a head tilt or rhythmic eye flickering
  • Allowing the clinic to prioritize care and prepare diagnostic tools before you arrive

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cerebellar disorders typically manifest as an intention tremor, wide-based stance, and incoordination (dysmetria) of the head, trunk, and limbs. Brain stem disorders may cause cranial nerve dysfunction, postural disturbances, or vestibular dysfunction, potentially leading to weakness, proprioceptive gait disturbances, and impaired consciousness. Spinal cord disorders can produce weakness, motor dysfunction, or proprioceptive deficits of the limbs, including dysmetria or reduced proprioceptive placing in more than one limb, without affecting cerebral function or head coordination. Neuromuscular disorders can also lead to unsteadiness and falling over. Some inherited conditions, such as fucosidosis in English Springer Spaniels, can manifest with ataxia, personality changes, dysphonia, dysphagia, hearing/visual deficits, and seizures, typically progressing from 6 to >24 months of age, ultimately carrying a poor prognosis.

Chapter: Neurology, Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1316)

faqs.question: Could my dog have been poisoned?
faqs.answer: Yes, certain toxins like xylitol, marijuana, or rodenticides can cause sudden loss of balance and incoordination. You should seek emergency care immediately if poisoning is suspected.

faqs.question: What is 'Old Dog Vestibular Disease'?
faqs.answer: This is a common condition affecting balance in senior dogs, often causing a sudden head tilt and stumbling. While it can look like a stroke, a veterinarian must diagnose it to rule out more serious neurological issues.

faqs.question: How should I move my wobbly dog to the car?
faqs.answer: Use a large towel as a sling under their belly to support their weight and prevent them from falling. For smaller dogs, carrying them in a secure crate is the safest option to prevent further injury.
